questions: At **CyanGate LLC**, you led the development of the **JSON-to-JSON AI Agentic System**, which involved integrating multiple LLM providers like OpenAI, Copilot, Gemini, and Anthropic—only to later see framework shifts or model updates (e.g., OpenAI’s GPT-4 Turbo or Anthropic’s Claude 3) that likely required architectural pivots. **Walk me through a specific instance where a mid-project change—like a new model release, a client’s sudden shift in requirements, or a framework deprecation—forced you to scrap or heavily modify weeks of work.** How did you communicate this to stakeholders (e.g., your team, the client, or management)? What was your emotional reaction in the moment, and how did you reframe the situation to stay productive?

Your **Atlassian Jira Intelligence System** project at CyanGate was built before MCP integration became standard, meaning you had to later adapt or even rebuild parts of the system to align with new platform capabilities. **Describe a time when you had to abandon a ‘working’ solution you’d invested significant time in (e.g., the Power Automate workflows or Copilot Studio conversational flows) because a better approach emerged.** How did you balance the frustration of ‘wasted effort’ with the need to adopt the new method? Did you advocate for incremental migration, or did you push for a full rewrite? How did you ensure the team stayed motivated during the transition?

Zephlen’s culture emphasizes *‘Radical Candor & Low Ego,’* where ideas are debated rigorously and decisions are made based on merit—not tenure or title. In your **TEKNOFEST competitions** (e.g., the Gold Medal in NLP or Bronze in E-Commerce AI), you worked under tight deadlines with high stakes, likely facing moments where a teammate or judge challenged your technical approach. **Tell me about a time when you had to defend your solution’s tradeoffs (e.g., cost vs. accuracy, speed vs. robustness) in a high-pressure setting, only to later realize your initial approach was flawed.** How did you handle the feedback? Did you pivot immediately, or did you need time to process it? How did that experience shape how you engage in technical debates today?
